semantics: The process-wide environment and argv/arg0-derived launch context that is sanitized, augmented, and then reused by later startup and runtime code.
The discovered installation layout, CODEX_HOME location, bundled assets, helper binaries, and machine-local installation facts shared across startup and maintenance flows.
The versioned shared helper-binary materialization state under codex_home that later execution and sandbox paths rely on.
The captured shell snapshot and validated execution-environment objects used to reproduce local or remote command environments across sessions and executors.
Normalized host identity and platform probe results such as hostname, shell, locale, editor, pager, and cloud-environment facts reused by diagnostics and runtime decisions.
The merged, validated effective configuration assembled from managed, cloud, user, project, thread, and CLI layers with provenance.
The resolved experimental-feature and startup feature-enablement state that gates runtime behavior and can be surfaced or updated through server APIs.
The compiled permission profiles, sandbox mode, filesystem/network ACLs, and related enforcement policy shared by sessions, tools, and transports.
The runtime-visible catalog of executable tools and their normalized schemas, exposure rules, and metadata used for dispatch and prompt assembly.
The resolved plugin, marketplace, hook, and skill configuration plus loaded skill/plugin metadata shared across startup, prompt assembly, and execution.
The materialized MCP declarations, runtime server metadata, and contribution overlays used for connection setup, routing, and prompt/tool exposure.
The merged bundled, cached, local-provider, and remotely fetched model inventory and presets used for picker, routing, and turn execution.
The active authentication mode and loaded credential state selected from storage or environment, including refresh and mode restrictions.
The persisted token payloads, JWT/account metadata, and revocation/restore state backing login and whoami behavior.
The stable per-installation identity used to distinguish this machine/runtime instance across auth, backend, and telemetry flows.
The Windows sandbox local-account and protected-credential state used to run isolated commands safely.
The shared SQLite-backed runtime handle and opened databases that provide durable local state services to higher layers.
The durable rollout transcript and thread-store persistence layer used for resume, reconstruction, metadata sync, archive, and repair.
The structured thread metadata, reconciliation, spawn-edge, and listing state maintained in SQLite for thread ownership and browsing.
The cached and refreshable cloud-configuration bundle state used to hydrate startup policy and remote-backed settings.
The refreshed connector/app directory and workspace enablement state that determines which integrations are visible and usable.
The current account, plan, usage, and rate-limit snapshot fetched from backends and replayed into UI and reconnecting clients.
The cached release/update availability state used by doctor, TUI startup, and daemon-managed updater flows.
The live app-server transport/listener/connection registry and per-connection routing state that all RPC handling depends on.
The persisted and live remote-control desired state, pairing/enrollment records, and reconnecting remote-session state.
The exec-server listener, client, process-control, and environment-discovery runtime state shared across request processing and execution.
The live MCP runtime sessions and transport connections maintained for tool routing and integration access.
The user-facing frontend session state including startup decisions, terminal ownership, visible chat/transcript state, and loaded thread view.
The in-memory registry of active threads and reconstructed thread runtimes that stabilizes ownership across resumes, forks, and UI switching.
The long-lived session object and shared services that own turn submission, event delivery, approvals, persistence, and runtime configuration.
The mutable session-wide state that survives across turns, including conversation history, token/rate-limit accounting, connector selections, and sticky grants.
The mutable active-turn coordination state including pending approvals, waiters, mailbox-delivery phase, and per-turn permission/review flags.
The buffered pending-input and mailbox queues that coordinate user steering, inter-agent delivery, and turn scheduling.
The immutable per-turn context snapshot derived from session settings, environments, permissions, model metadata, and runtime services before model calls.
The session-scoped auto-compaction window state that tracks context-growth baselines and rollover across turns and resumptions.
The assembled prompt fragments, injected context, normalized history, and final prompt state produced for the current turn.
The live approval/review decision state for guarded actions, including requested, granted, denied, and sticky approval outcomes.
The tracked process-execution state and exit/failure snapshots for spawned commands and related unified-exec handles.
The in-memory registry of active agents, spawn reservations, path/nickname allocation, and concurrency/residency limits for multi-agent work.
The persisted and live background workflow state for agent-job batches, worker progress, and other longer-lived asynchronous pipelines.
The durable and runtime state for memory extraction/consolidation jobs and filesystem-backed memory artifacts.
The projected user-visible thread state derived from events and persisted records, including item views, statuses, summaries, and replayed token usage.
The per-connection shutdown acceptance flags, running-handler tracking, and cleanup-task registries used for graceful drain and teardown.
The shared outbound networking/proxy/TLS/cookie and local-IPC policy state that governs how clients and transports may connect.
The host-seeded and extension-owned typed attachment stores that let extensions keep shared runtime state across lifecycle callbacks.
The global tracing/logging/metrics context and stable session-turn-auth-model-tool tags attached to emitted telemetry throughout runtime.
The in-memory and batched analytics facts/events pipeline that reduces observations into product events and ships or captures them.
The durable runtime log and feedback artifact store used for diagnostics, support capture, and later investigation.
Shared outbound HTTP/WebSocket client instances with their connection pools, cookie jars, and retry-capable transport state reused across backend, cloud, and integration calls.
Per-resource/per-thread ordering queues that serialize conflicting RPC or session requests while allowing unrelated work to proceed concurrently.
The live token-budget and remaining-context-window accounting used to shape prompt assembly, compaction decisions, and user-visible budget indicators across turns.
The persisted and live per-thread goal state, including current goals and related structured goal metadata surfaced in sessions and UI.
The background file-watch and invalidation state that monitors skill sources and marks cached skill data stale for later reload.
Background startup refresh tasks and their cached latest results for cloud config, model/catalog sync, plugin snapshots, memories guards, and similar early-runtime hydrators.
The daemon’s long-lived updater loop state, including periodic check scheduling, managed-installer refresh progress, and pending restart/re-exec decisions.
The locally cached curated core-plugin snapshot and shared-plugin path mapping reused across startup sync, plugin resolution, and offline/runtime reads.
Persistent local caches for fetched remote catalogs such as connectors, models, and plugin/marketplace metadata that survive restarts and seed startup hydration.
The session prewarm/warm-start state prepared ahead of turns and then reused by turn execution to avoid rebuilding expensive session resources.
The queued/redacted feedback submission state, including pending attachments and thread/log references, held until feedback is uploaded or persisted for retry.
The active rollout-tracing capture state that accumulates deterministic execution traces for later replay, export, or debugging.
The durable coordination/lease state for one-time or resumable metadata backfills and startup repair work that blocks or gates higher-level initialization.
The runtime-ready outbound authentication material derived from auth state, such as bearer headers, agent assertions, or SigV4 signing context, reused by backend clients and transports.
The live per-thread realtime conversation/session state, including active start/append/stop lifecycle and associated transport/session bookkeeping.
The registry of long-lived interactive command/process sessions, including PTY handles, stdin/write channels, resize/terminate control, and streamed output subscribers.
The cached resolved outputs of extension contributors—such as prompt fragments, MCP overlays, tool/thread lifecycle contributions, and user instructions—reused across thread and turn assembly.
The persistent record of previously imported external-session sources and their normalized ingestion outcomes used to avoid duplicate imports and support resume/inspection.
The persisted and live daemon/app-server process-discovery state, including PID files, readiness probes, and launched detached server instances reused across client attach and restart flows.
Per-connection initialization/handshake state that gates which RPC methods are allowed before a transport session is fully initialized.
The per-thread and per-connection listener/subscription registry that tracks who is watching which thread or process streams for ordered notifications.
The session-visible cache of user-run shell command records and approved command-prefix memory that is reused for prompt context and later turns.
The mutable per-session or per-thread selection/enablement state for which connectors/apps are currently chosen for use and prompt injection.
The live concurrency, cancellation, and in-flight execution coordination state for parallel tool calls and other dispatched runtime tasks.
Long-lived watcher/timer task state such as code-mode timers and other runtime background loops that persist across requests until shutdown.
The accumulated per-turn and per-thread token-usage accounting buffer that is persisted, replayed to reconnecting clients, and surfaced in UI/account views.
The runtime hook execution state and recent hook results/notifications used to mediate tool lifecycle events and expose hook summaries to clients.
The startup and runtime guard state that tracks whether memories functionality is currently allowed, blocked, or degraded based on prerequisite checks.
The separate append-only global message-history file/state that records cross-session message history outside per-thread rollout storage.
The encrypted local secrets persistence used to retain sensitive integration or runtime secrets across startup and execution flows.
The durable graph of thread spawn relationships and lifecycle edges exposed independently of live thread metadata for agent-tree queries and teardown consistency.
